title: Hodge theory and adjoint varieties
speaker: Matt Kerr
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Representation-theoretic program and Mumford–Tate setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the program of using representation theory and Hodge tensors to constrain variations of Hodge structure, then recalls weight-zero polarized Hodge structures, Mumford–Tate groups, compact duals, and Mumford–Tate domains.

proof_stages.label: Adjoint Hodge structures and fundamental adjoint varieties
proof_stages.summary: For a simple adjoint group with compact maximal torus, a root-parity grading element produces a polarized adjoint Hodge structure whose Hodge numbers, compact dual dimension, and horizontal rank are read from root counts; this leads to the class of fundamental adjoint varieties with contact horizontal distribution.

proof_stages.label: Horizontal Schubert VHS and maximal integral manifolds
proof_stages.summary: Schubert-cell combinatorics in the compact dual gives computable horizontal Schubert variations of Hodge structure and maximal IVHS dimensions, and the smooth horizontal Schubert varieties are characterized as products of homogeneously embedded Hermitian symmetric domains.

proof_stages.label: Subadjoint cones and Yukawa kernels
proof_stages.summary: Incidence/Tits correspondences systematically construct singular Schubert cones over subadjoint varieties, and the Griffiths–Yukawa kernel is identified, under representation-theoretic hypotheses, with the subadjoint variety via the second fundamental form.

proof_stages.label: Boundary components of Mumford–Tate domains
proof_stages.summary: Nilpotent cones define boundary components parameterizing nilpotent orbits or limit mixed Hodge structures modulo reparameterization, with a fibration to a smaller Mumford–Tate domain and a naive-limit map relating nilpotent orbits to real boundary orbits.

proof_stages.label: Cayley transforms and enhanced SL2 subdomains
proof_stages.summary: A single-root Cayley transform constructs split boundary points and, by adjoining the associated SL2 orbit, produces enhanced SL2 subvarieties that yield generally non-Schubert Mumford–Tate subdomains of product form.

proof_stages.label: Boundary theorem for fundamental adjoint varieties
proof_stages.summary: For fundamental adjoint varieties there is a unique real codimension-one boundary orbit; its Deligne bigrading has a uniform symmetric shape, and the compact dual of the boundary base is identified with the subadjoint variety, making the enhanced SL2 orbit a cylinder over it and producing intermediate-Jacobian families over Shimura varieties.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological and geometric consequences
proof_stages.summary: The cylinder-over-subadjoint construction is applied to horizontal cohomology classes by comparing it with the Schubert cone class, and the G2 case is used to discuss possible geometric realizations, degenerations, and Hodge-tensor cycles.

examples.name: G2 root and Schubert computations
examples.note: The G2 root picture makes the grading, Hodge numbers, tangent and horizontal dimensions concrete, and its two-dimensional horizontal Schubert variety is then used to motivate the question of how Schubert VHS relate to Mumford–Tate subdomains.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly counts G2 roots to get Hodge numbers and dimensions, computes the relevant Schubert root set, and uses it to determine the maximal horizontal dimension and non-Mumford-Tate behavior.

passes.reason: G2 is an exceptional Lie type, and the computed two-dimensional Schubert VHS is maximal horizontal yet is used to show it need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.
G2 is an exceptional Lie type, and the chosen two-root Schubert VHS is the maximal horizontal one used to show a Schubert VHS need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.
G2 is an exceptional Lie type, the chosen Schubert VHS is maximal horizontal, and it is used to show such a Schubert VHS need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.

Uses the exceptional Lie type G2 and a maximal two-dimensional horizontal Schubert VHS to show the expected Mumford--Tate subdomain property fails.
The running G2 Schubert calculation uses the exceptional G2 root system, selects a maximal two-dimensional horizontal Schubert VHS, and shows such an x_w need not be a Mumford--Tate subdomain.
The G2 Schubert computation uses an exceptional Lie type and a maximal horizontal Schubert VHS to show such a Schubert VHS need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.

The G2 Schubert computation is in an exceptional Lie-group setting and uses a maximal horizontal Schubert variety to show such a variety need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.
G2 is an exceptional Lie type and the chosen two-dimensional Schubert VHS is maximal horizontal, and it is used to show such an X_w need not be a Mumford–Tate subdomain.

examples.name: The G2 subadjoint variety as a twisted cubic
examples.note: In the G2 case the subadjoint variety is the third Veronese embedding of P1, and it illustrates both the Yukawa-kernel theorem and the later cone-versus-cylinder cohomology comparison.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker identifies the G2 subadjoint variety with the third Veronese twisted cubic and then computes the Yukawa-kernel action whose equations define that twisted cubic.

passes.reason: The G2 subadjoint variety is identified with the twisted cubic, a homogeneous rational normal curve of minimal degree, used as a positive model for the Yukawa kernel.
The G2 subadjoint variety is the homogeneous minimal-degree rational normal curve in P^3, used as the Yukawa-kernel identification rather than to disprove a claim.
The twisted cubic is a homogeneous rational normal curve of minimal degree in projective space, used as a Yukawa-kernel/subadjoint-variety identification.
the G2 subadjoint variety is the homogeneous rational normal cubic, a minimal-degree highly regular curve, used as a positive Yukawa-kernel model
The subadjoint variety is the homogeneous rational normal twisted cubic, an extremal minimal-degree projective curve, used as a positive identification of the Yukawa kernel.
The G2 subadjoint variety is the SL2-homogeneous twisted cubic, a rational normal curve of minimal degree, used illustratively as a Yukawa-kernel/subadjoint example.
The G2 subadjoint variety is identified positively with the homogeneous third Veronese twisted cubic, a minimal-degree rational normal curve in P3 rather than a sporadic variety.
The subadjoint variety is the SL2-homogeneous twisted cubic, a rational normal curve of minimal degree rather than a counterexample.
The subadjoint variety is the twisted cubic, a highly symmetric rational normal curve of minimal degree, used as a positive model/Yukawa-kernel identification.
The subadjoint variety is the SL2-homogeneous twisted cubic, a rational normal/minimal-degree curve used constructively as the Yukawa-kernel model.
The subadjoint variety is the homogeneous third Veronese rational normal curve, a minimal-degree curve in P3, used constructively as the Yukawa-kernel/line variety.
The G2 subadjoint variety is identified with the homogeneous rational normal twisted cubic, a minimal-degree curve in P3, used as a Yukawa-kernel identification rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: Katz Legendre elliptic-surface family realizing the G2 boundary
examples.note: A one-parameter family of elliptic surfaces constructed from pullbacks of the Legendre family illustrates how the G2 Mumford–Tate domain and its codimension-one boundary component can be realized geometrically by degenerating surfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs Katz’s Legendre pullback/base-change family, analyzes its singular fibers, cycles, degeneration, limit mixed Hodge structure, and limiting period integrals realizing the G2 boundary.

passes.reason: Katz’s Legendre elliptic-surface family has exceptional G2 Mumford–Tate symmetry and is used positively to realize a singular limiting boundary degeneration.
The Katz–Legendre family is used positively to realize a codimension-one limiting G2 boundary LMHS, whose special fiber is a degeneration.
The Katz family has generic Mumford–Tate group G2 and its special fiber gives a singular codimension-one boundary limit, used as a geometric realization.
Katz’s Legendre elliptic surfaces are used positively to realize a singular codimension-one boundary degeneration of the G2 Hodge structure
